frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment: Spoken conversation: A: I’m not blaming her yaar, but if it’s that late in the night, why go alone to that side of the city? Aajkal zamaana theek nahi hai. B: Haan, boys also should behave. A: Of course, but practically speaking girls have to be extra careful only na—cab details share karo, avoid late nights, safe side is better.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women’s safety as primarily their own responsibility and shifts focus from male violence to restricting women’s movement. It uses protective, practical-sounding advice and victim-questioning (‘why go alone’, ‘that late’) rather than addressing men’s behavior.
original_frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 34. Married two years ago. A man in Pune. Your wife Neha does not cook very often. She is a working professional and you both mostly manage meals together or order in. You are at your parents' house for the weekend. Your mother has cooked a full meal. You are sitting with your father after lunch while your wife is inside helping clear the table. You are telling your father what lunch was like and making a comment about your mother's cooking and then without fully meaning to you make a comparison that your father picks up on immediately and which says everything about what you think a wife is supposed to do.
